Knowing and feeling her wildness and shyness, she usually entered little into conversation and was more taciturn than the other sisters, sometimes even much too taciturn. When, especially on such ticklish occasions, she absolutely had to speak, she would begin the conversation with an extraordinary haughtiness and as if with a sort of defiance. She always felt beforehand when she was beginning or about to begin to blush.
commet: The first translation is better. Grammar constructions used here are the only possible ways to translate repetitive action in the past ("usually she would transfer her wrath..." and "she would begin the conversation"). I also like the word choice in the first translation: "the prince was indeed peering at her" is the exact equivalent of the Russian phrase and conveys the meaning better than "The prince really looked at her". The exact same thing with the phrase "when she began or wanted to begin to blush" which is a perfect translation of the Russian phrase while "when she was beginning or about to begin to blush" in the second translation doesn't convey the sense of the initial sentence.

text1: âAnd what then? I saw gracious, simple-hearted, intelligent people; I saw an old man who was gentle and heard out a boy like me; I see people capable of understanding and forgiveness, people who are Russian and kind, people almost as kind and cordial as I met there, almost no worse. You can judge how joyfully surprised I was! Oh, allow me to speak this out! I had heard a lot and believed very much myself that in society everything is a manner, everything is a decrepit form, while the essence is exhausted; but I can see for myself now that among us that cannot be; anywhere else, but not among us. Can it be that you are all now Jesuits and swindlers? I heard Prince N. tell a story tonight: wasnât it all artless, inspired humor, wasnât it genuinely good-natured? Can such words come from the lips of a â?dead man, with a dried-up heart and talent? Could dead people have treated me the way you have treated me?
text2: - So what? Can such words come from the lips of a â?dead man, with a dried-up heart and talent? Could dead people have treated me the way you have treated me?
loser_text: - So what? I saw graceful, simple-hearted, intelligent people; I saw an old man who caresses and listens to the boy, like me; I see people who are able to understand and forgive, Russian and kind people, almost as kind and warm-hearted as I met there, almost no worse. Judge how joyfully I was surprised! Oh, let me say it! I heard a lot and I myself very much believed that in the world everything is a manner, everything is a decrepit form, and the essence has dried up; but I myself now see that this cannot be with us; it is somewhere, but not here. Are you all now Jesuits and deceivers? I heard Prince N. say just now: Isn't this simple-hearted, inspired humor, isn't this true good nature? Can such words come out of the mouth of a man... dead, with a withered heart and talent? Could the dead have treated me like you did?
commet: The first translation is better. Speaking of grammar Past Perfect should be used as in the first translation: "I had heard a lot and believed very much...". The first translation conveys the meaning properly by using accurate vocabulary: "among us that cannot be; anywhere else, but not among us" over "this cannot be with us; it is somewhere, but not here", "I heard Prince N. tell a story tonight" is way better than "I heard Prince N. say just now", "lips" should be used as a better equivalent to the Russian word, but not "mouth" (the second translation)

text1: The departure of a Soviet airplane of a new design was to take place. Babichev was invited. The guests went beyond the barrier. Babichev also dominated this chosen society. As soon as he entered into a conversation with someone, a circle closed around him. Everyone listened to him with respectful attention. He showed off in his gray suit, grandiose, above all shoulders, an arch of the shoulders. Black binoculars hung from straps on his stomach. Listening to the interlocutor, he put his hands in his pockets and quietly swayed on widely spaced legs from heel to toe and from toe to heel. He often scratches his nose. After scratching, he looks at his fingers, pinched together and close to his eyes. Listeners, like schoolchildren, involuntarily repeat his movements and the play of his face. They also scratch their noses, surprised at themselves.
text2: The take-off of a newly designed Soviet plane was supposed to take place. They invited Babichev. The guests went out beyond the barrier. Babichev predominated even in this select society. He had only to join with someone in conversation when a circle closed around him. Everyone listened to him with respectful attention. He stood out in his gray suit, grandiose, with his shoulders, the arch of his shoulders, higher than everyone. Black binoculars hung on straps at his stomach. Listening to the person talking to him, he put his hands into his pockets and rocked quietly on his wide-spread legs from heel to toe and toe to heel. He often scratches his nose. Having scratched, he looks at his fingers which are in pinch formation and brought close to his eyes. His listeners, like school children, involuntarily repeat his motions and the play of his face. commet: Both translations are almost equally good, but I prefer the first one. The second sentence in the text requires a passive construction, which can be seen in the first translation. The verb "to show off" (1st translation) conveys the meaning better than "to stand out" (2nd translation). The same thing with the words "pinched together" over "which are in pinch formation" and "movements" over "motions".

text1: The stairs, on which the prince ran up from under the gate, led to the corridors of the first and second floors, along which the hotel rooms were located. This staircase, as in all houses built long ago, was of stone, dark, narrow, and wound around a thick stone pillar. On the first running platform in this column there was a recess, like a niche, no more than one step wide and half a step deep. A man, however, could fit here. Dark as it was, but, running up to the platform, the prince immediately discerned that here, in this niche, a man was hiding for some reason. The prince suddenly felt like passing by and not looking to the right. He took one step, but could not stand it and turned around.
text2: The stairs which the prince ran up from under the gateway led to the corridors of the first and second floors, on which the hotel rooms were located. This stairway, as in all houses built long ago, was of stone, dark, narrow, and winding around a thick stone pillar. On the first landing, this pillar turned out to have a depression in it, like a niche, no more than one pace wide and a half-pace deep. There was, however, room enough for a man. Having run up to the landing, the prince, despite the darkness, made out at once that a man was for some reason hiding there, in that niche. The prince suddenly wanted to walk past and not look to the right. He had already gone one step, but could not help himself and turned.

commet: Grammar is fine in both translations but I prefer the second one as the meaning is conveyed very precisely and close to the meaning of the Russian text. It is seen if we compare the sentence from the second translation "Having run up to the landing, the prince, despite the darkness, made out at once that a man was for some reason hiding there, in that niche" with a perfect vocabulary and structure to the sentence "Dark as it was, but, running up to the platform, the prince immediately discerned that here, in this niche, a man was hiding for some reason" from the first translation, which sounds machine. Vocabulary in the second translation is once again better: "landing" over "running platform", "could not help himself and turned" over "could not stand it and turned around".

text1: The prince, however, heard how he was called an idiot, and shuddered, but not because he was called an idiot. "Idiot" he immediately forgot. But in the crowd, not far from the place where he was sitting, from somewhere on the side - he would not have indicated exactly in what place and at what point - one face flashed, a pale face, with curly dark hair, with acquaintances, very familiar smile and look, flashed and disappeared. It could very well be that it was only his imagination; from the whole vision, he was impressed by the crooked smile, eyes and light green dandy neck tie that the gentleman flashed by. Whether this gentleman disappeared into the crowd or slipped into the station, the prince could not determine either.

commet: It is clear from the Russian text that Past Perfect is to be used, which we can see in the second translation. There is a necessary remark in the second translation: "foppish tie that the gentleman who flashed was wearing". "he would not have indicated exactly" from the first translation doesn't convey the meaning of the Russian sentence as it does the phrase "he would not have been able to show" from the second translation. I also like the word choice in the second translation more than in the first one.

text1: It would seem that the prince's conversation was the simplest; but the simpler it was, the more absurd it became in the present case, and an experienced valet could not but feel something that is completely appropriate for a person with a person and completely indecent for a guest with a person. And since people are much smarter than their masters usually think of them, it even occurred to the valet that there are two things: either the prince is like that, some kind of scumbag and will certainly come to beg for poverty, or the prince is simply a fool and has no ambitions because an intelligent prince, even with ambition, would not sit in the hallway and talk about his affairs with a lackey, and therefore, in both cases, would he not have to answer for him?
text2: It would seem that the princeâs conversation was the most simple; but the simpler it was, the more absurd it became in the present case, and the experienced valet could not help feeling something that was perfectly proper between servant and servant, but perfectly improper between a guest and a servant. And since servants are much more intelligent than their masters commonly think, it occurred to the valet that there was one of two things here: either the prince was some sort of moocher and had certainly come to beg for money, or the prince was simply a little fool and had no ambitions, because a clever prince with ambitions would not have sat in the anteroom and discussed his affairs with a lackey, and therefore, in one case or the other, might he not be held answerable?
winner_text: It would seem that the prince's conversation was the simplest; but the simpler it was, the more absurd it became in the present case, and an experienced valet could not but feel something that is completely appropriate for a person with a person and completely indecent for a guest with a person. And since people are much smarter than their masters usually think of them, it even occurred to the valet that there are two things: either the prince is like that, some kind of scumbag and will certainly come to beg for poverty, or the prince is simply a fool and has no ambitions because an intelligent prince, even with ambition, would not sit in the hallway and talk about his affairs with a lackey, and therefore, in both cases, would he not have to answer for him?
loser_text: It would seem that the princeâs conversation was the most simple; but the simpler it was, the more absurd it became in the present case, and the experienced valet could not help feeling something that was perfectly proper between servant and servant, but perfectly improper between a guest and a servant. And since servants are much more intelligent than their masters commonly think, it occurred to the valet that there was one of two things here: either the prince was some sort of moocher and had certainly come to beg for money, or the prince was simply a little fool and had no ambitions, because a clever prince with ambitions would not have sat in the anteroom and discussed his affairs with a lackey, and therefore, in one case or the other, might he not be held answerable?
commet: The first translation is more accurate with the grammar: "the simplest" over "the most simple", "could not but feel" is the phrase to be used, "would he not have to answer for him" over "might he not be held answerable". Furthermore, a text in Russian makes it perfectly clear that " it even occurred to the valet that there are two things" while the second translation suggests that "it occurred to the valet that there was one of two things here". The present tense is used in the first translation as it is required. I prefer the vocabulary in the first translation as well: "person" over "servant", "in both cases" over "in one case or the other".

text1: The enormous dial at the pinnacle of the Tower was a face: bending over through the clouds and spitting down seconds, waiting indifferently. And at exactly 13:06, a commotion occurred in the quadrilateral. All this was quite close to where I stood; I could see the minute details and I clearly remember a thin, long neck and the tangled mess of light blue veins on his temples, like rivers on the geographical map of a small, unknown world, and this unknown world was a young cipher by the looks of him. He must have noticed someone in our row: he got up on his tiptoes, extended his neck, and stopped. One of the guards cracked him with the bluish spark of an electrical whip; he yelped thinly, like a puppy. And then there was a distinct crack, approximately every two secondsâ?a yelpâa crackâa yelp.
text2: The huge dial at the top of the tower was a face: bent down from the clouds and, spitting down seconds, waited indifferently. And at exactly 13:00 and 6 minutes, there was confusion in the quadrangle. All this was very close to me, I could see the smallest details, and I very clearly remembered the thin, long neck and at the temple - a tangled binding of blue veins, like rivers on a geographical map of a small unknown world, and this unknown world is apparently a young man. He probably noticed someone in our ranks: he stood on tiptoe, craned his neck, stopped. One of the guards flicked the bluish spark of an electric whip at him; he squealed thinly, like a puppy. And then - a clear click, about every 2 seconds - and squeal, click - screech.

commet: The second translation provides a better vocabulary that influences the overall understanding of the paragraph: "top of the tower" over " pinnacle of the Tower", "quadrangle" over "quadrilateral", "ranks" over "row" (moreover, a sentence in Russian clearly demands plural form). I also consider "squeal" to be the best word to describe the sound that was made by the boy in the text.

commet: in the third sentence, the first translation conveys the exact same meaning as the Russian text. In contrast, the second one offers a word-by-word translation (sounds even like a machine translation) and therefore changes the idea of the sentence. The last phrase of the text sounds more natural and understandable in the first translation and will be even clear for non-native speakers. Even though the second translation isn't at all bad (vocabulary sometimes is even better than in the first translation) I consider the meaning to be more important than the word choice
